title: Vega-1 Quadcopter
tagline: A 5-inch carbon freestyle quad built for low-latency FPV.
summary: A hand-tuned 5" freestyle quadcopter on a carbon frame, running a custom PID profile for crisp propwash handling and sub-10ms video latency.

sections.heading: Design goals
body: Vega-1 started as an exercise in reducing control latency end to end — from stick input to motor response to the video feed reaching my goggles. The target was a quad that felt telepathic in freestyle without becoming twitchy in wind.
I settled on a 22mm-thick arm carbon frame to survive hard tumbles, paired with 2207 motors that give enough authority for power loops while staying efficient on a 6S pack.

sections.heading: Tuning notes
body: The stock Betaflight tune had noticeable propwash oscillation on descents. Dropping D-term filtering delay and raising the dynamic notch Q cleaned up the response without introducing hot motors.
Rates are set to 900 deg/s with a soft center for precision on gap lines, then aggressive expo past 70% stick for flips.

title: Aether VTOL
tagline: A tilt-rotor fixed-wing that hovers on takeoff and cruises on wing.
summary: A vertical-takeoff fixed-wing aircraft that transitions from multirotor hover to efficient forward flight, extending range to over 40 minutes.

sections.heading: Why VTOL
body: Fixed-wing gives efficiency and range, but needs a runway. Multirotors are convenient but power-hungry. Aether is my attempt at the best of both: it takes off vertically, tilts its front rotors forward, and transitions onto the wing for cruise.
The hardest part is the transition window — the moment where the wing is not yet producing full lift but the rotors are tilting away from vertical thrust.

sections.heading: Transition logic
body: ArduPilot handles the QuadPlane state machine, but the airspeed threshold for transition had to be tuned carefully against a real pitot reading. Too early and it mushes; too late and it wastes battery hovering.
Current builds transition cleanly at 14 m/s with a 3-second tilt ramp.

title: Ascent-2 Sounding Rocket
tagline: A dual-deploy high-power rocket targeting 10,000 ft on an L-class motor.
summary: A carbon-airframe high-power rocket with dual-deploy recovery and an onboard flight computer logging the full ascent to apogee.

sections.heading: Airframe
body: Ascent-2 uses a filament-wound carbon airframe for stiffness at transonic speeds, with G10 fiberglass fins bonded through the wall to the motor mount for shear strength.
The nose cone houses the recovery electronics bay, isolated from the motor by a bulkhead rated well above expected ejection pressures.

sections.heading: Recovery
body: Dual-deploy keeps drift manageable: a drogue chute pops at apogee to bring the rocket down fast and stable, then the main deploys at 700 ft AGL for a soft landing.
The flight computer runs a Kalman filter fusing barometric and accelerometer data so ejection charges fire on true apogee, not on noisy pressure spikes.

title: Helios Flight Controller
tagline: A custom STM32 flight controller board designed from schematic to firmware.
summary: A ground-up flight controller PCB built around an STM32H7, dual IMUs, and a barometer — the shared brain behind several of my airframes.

sections.heading: From schematic to board
body: Helios is my first flight controller designed entirely in KiCad — power tree, sensor routing, and a 4-layer stackup with a dedicated ground plane to keep the IMUs quiet.
Two IMUs sit on soft-mounted pads with slightly different orientations, so firmware can vote against a bad sensor and reject frame resonance.

sections.heading: Firmware
body: The board boots both a stripped-down custom firmware for bench testing and PX4 for the larger airframes. Getting the gyro loop stable at 8 kHz meant carefully budgeting DMA transfers so the SPI reads never stalled the control loop.
Next milestone is closing the loop on a test bench gimbal before it flies anything expensive.

title: Nimbus Autopilot Stack
tagline: A telemetry + mission software layer that ties my aircraft together.
summary: A ground-station and telemetry stack that streams live flight data, plans autonomous missions, and replays logs for post-flight tuning.

sections.heading: The problem
body: Every airframe I build produces logs, but they lived in different formats and tools. Nimbus is my attempt to unify them: one telemetry pipeline, one dashboard, one log format I actually enjoy reading.
A Rust service ingests the MAVLink stream, writes it to a time-series database, and pushes live updates to a React map interface.

sections.heading: Mission planning
body: Beyond monitoring, Nimbus lets me draw waypoint missions on a map and upload them to an ArduPilot or PX4 vehicle, then watch execution against the plan in real time.
The replay mode has already saved me hours of tuning by scrubbing through exactly where a tune went wrong.

sections.heading: yes
body: no but yes
